name: Kukeri / Surva Festival (International Festival of the Masquerade Games)
dates: January 16–25 (32nd edition in 2026); main kukeri parades on weekends of January 17–18 and January 24–25
description: The largest Kukeri festival in Bulgaria and the biggest masquerade event on the Balkan Peninsula, held in Pernik (~40 min drive from Sofia). Around 6,000 participants (~100 groups) from every folklore region of Bulgaria and from Europe, Asia, and Africa compete. The International Federation of Carnival Cities accepted Pernik as a full member in 1995. In 2009 the festival was proclaimed the European Capital of Survas and Mummers. UNESCO recognized it as Intangible Heritage of Humanity in June 2009.

name: Kukeri Festival — Kukerlandia
dates: February 19–22 (2026 dates; annual event in mid-to-late February)
description: Kukerlandia is the major annual Kukeri masquerade festival held in Yambol, eastern Bulgaria. Features elaborately costumed Kukeri performers from across Bulgaria competing in parades through the town centre. Eastern Bulgarian Kukeri traditions (Surva rituals held around Easter) are especially well represented here.

name: Kukeri Festival
dates: Around March 1 (Baba Marta / Martenitsa); first Sunday of March annually
description: One of the most traditional Kukeri festivals in Bulgaria, held in the Rhodope mountain village of Shiroka Laka. The festival preserves authentic local Kukeri costumes and rituals specific to the Rhodope region. Participants parade through the village's characteristic stone-paved streets in enormous fur costumes with large bells and carved wooden or fur masks.

name: Kukeri Festival
description: Kukeri festival in the mountain town of Razlog in southwestern Bulgaria. Features traditional masquerade parades with local kukeri groups. Razlog is noted as one of the main centers for observing Kukeri traditions alongside Pernik, Yambol, and Shiroka Laka.

name: Kukeri — Overview and Origins
dates: Winter months between New Year and Lent (January–March; initially performed at night, now during the day)
description: The Kukeri are one of the oldest folk rituals in Bulgaria, with Thracian roots (pre-Christian origin). The word 'Kuker' may derive from Latin 'cucullus' (hood). A Kuker wears an elaborate costume of fur, feathers, and other materials, plus an intimidating animal mask (typically resembling a ram, goat, bull, or bear — sometimes double-faced to symbolize good and evil) and large copper or brass bells hung from the waist. The bells and scary costumes are used to chase away evil spirits and bring prosperity, good health, and luck. Red in the costumes symbolizes revival and the sun; black represents earth; white stands for light and water. The kukeri rituals initially occurred at night. Similar traditions exist in Romania (mascați), North Macedonia (Babari), Serbia, and Greece.
